EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widely scattered power outages were reported in isolated locations in New Hampshire due to strong winds.
Widely scattered power outages were reported in isolated locations in New Hampshire due to strong winds.

An arctic cold front moved through Northern New England on the afternoon of Jan. 13.  Arctic high pressure settled southeast from Central Canada Jan. 14 through Jan. 16.  Wind chill values of 33 to 50 degrees below zero were recorded during the peak of this outbreak on the morning of Jan. 15.

Low pressure developed in southwest Pennsylvania on the morning of December 23rd and moved northeast through western New York spreading rain into Maine and New Hampshire. Rainfall amounts of 1 to 2 inches were common with Pinkham Notch receiving 2.87 inches. This was enough rain to bring small rivers and streams over their banks and resulted in minor flooding.The Pemigewasset at Woodstock was observed above the 9 foot flood stage between 1100 pm on the 23rd to 300 am on the 24th, cresting at 9.39 feet at 1200 am on the 24th.

Low pressure developing off the mid Atlantic coast during the early morning hours of Tuesday, February 3, moved northeast through the day, reaching the New England coastal waters Tuesday night. Snow moved into southern New England Tuesday evening and overspread New Hampshire and Western Maine overnight.  Snow accumulated 1 to 3 inches in interior sections of Southern Maine and New Hampshire where precipitation mixed with sleet and freezing rain.  Snow accumulated 3 to 6 inches in the mountains and foothills.

A late season winter storm passing southeast of New England brought heavy snow to southwest New Hampshire, where snowfall totals of 5 to 10 inches were common.  Several minor accidents were reported, primarily in the Nashua and Manchester areas, otherwise there was little impact noted from this storm.Some specific snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters, included 10 inches in Peterborough; 8 inches in Francestown; 7 inches in Keene, Rindge, Nashua, and Milford; and 6 inches in Walpole, Winchester, Amherst, Merrimack, and South Weare.

Low pressure developed over North Carolina on the morning of Tuesday, March 16, and moved Northeast passing south of Cape Cod early on Wednesday, March 17. Low pressure continued Northeast through the Gulf of Maine Wednesday afternoon. Snow began across Southern New Hampshire and Southwest Maine on Tuesday afternoon and overspread the remainder of the forecast area through the evening hours. Snowfall ended around noon on Wednesday. Heaviest accumulations were observed across Southern New Hampshire and extreme Southwest Maine where 4 to 10 inches were recorded. Amounts in Central New Hampshire and Southern Interior Maine ranged from 1 to 4 inches with 1 inch or less reported in far northern zones.

Low pressure over the Great Lakes redeveloped off the mid Atlantic coast Friday evening, February 5, and tracked northeast along a stationary front Friday night and into the Gulf of Maine by early Saturday morning February 6. Snow began spreading into Southern New Hampshire and Maine on Friday morning. Snow changed to sleet and freezing rain in coastal and southern interior sections of Maine and New Hampshire where accumulations by Saturday morning measured 1 to 3 inches. Snowfall accumulations of 3 to 5 inches were recorded over the mountains and foothills.

Minor to moderate flooding occurred along the Contoocook and Piscataquog Rivers, as a result of a widespread 2 to 4 inch rainfall which took place over three days.  Locally as much as 4 to 6 inches of rain was reported.Flooding impacted several roads along both rivers, forcing closure of the more susceptible areas.  Some neighborhoods located in low lying areas were forced to evacuate.  No estimate of flood damage was available.

A slow moving cutoff low pressure system produced over 6 inches of rain in some locations of Southern New Hampshire, (West Hampstead 6.11 inches), flooding many small rivers and streams in the area. Rainfall began on Thursday, April 1, and continued through the morning of Friday, April 2. The Spicket, Lamprey and Oyster Rivers flooded. Especially hard hit was Salem, New Hampshire where many homes were evacuated due to the rising Spicket River and Paltrey Brook.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved into Western New Hampshire around 1030 pm EDT on the night of Monday, April 19.  Damage was caused by straight line winds.  National Weather Service spotters called in several reports of trees down across Grafton county.

Low pressure developed off the New England coast on the night of Sunday, April 4, and tracked through the Gulf of Maine through Monday night, April 5. Rain changed to snow over the higher elevations Sunday afternoon and gradually changed to snow in the notches and mountain valleys Sunday night. Snowfall totals by early Tuesday morning ranged from 2 to 12 inches with higher amounts likely over elevated terrain.

A lightning strike caused a fire that badly damaged a Bedford home in the middle of the night.  No one was injured when lightning struck the single-story home's basement, causing a series of electrical failures that started the fire.  The house was a total loss.
An isolated severe thunderstorm downed tree limbs and wires in Marlow.

A severe thunderstorm briefly produced penny sized hail in Manchester, but no damage was reported.  Another severe thunderstorm downed large tree limbs as it entered Hinsdale in western Cheshire County, and an amateur radio operator also observed a funnel cloud as the storm passed.

A Dover woman was seriously injured when she was struck by lightning while she walked her dog in the family's back yard.  After the strike, the woman was left unconscious, not breathing, and without a pulse.  Her husband was able to revive her with CPR, and she was taken to a nearby hospital for medical treatment.
A lightning strike ignited a fire that heavily damaged a home in Lee.

Lightning struck a home in Rochester and ignited a fire that caused extensive damage to the attic, roof, and second floor of the structure.

Lightning struck the chimney of an inn in Danbury causing an estimated $15,000-$20,000 in damage.  One of the cinder block chimneys was blown apart in the incident.  Numerous electronic devices were damaged in the strike, and flying debris caused damage to skylights and furniture, and the force of the strike shattered windows.

Lightning struck a garage and home in Wolfeboro and ignited a fire that burned the garage to the ground and damaged the siding on the house.  In addition the home's electrical system was damaged by the lightning strike.
Lightning strikes ignited two house fires in Walpole.

A weak tornado touched down near Steele Hill Road in Sanbornton and moved northeast. The tornado lifted off the ground as it moved across the northern part of the town of Laconia, then touched down again as it crossed Lake Winnipesaukee into the southeastern part of Meredith, just southeast of Pinnacle Hill.

Up to 3 inches of rain in 2.5 hours caused flash flooding of roads as well as washouts due to overloaded culverts. Portions of Route 135 in Dalton were washed out as well as portions of Route 3 in Lancaster.

Up to 3 inches of rain in 2.5 hours caused flash flooding of roads as well as washouts due to overloaded culverts. Portions of Route 135 in Dalton were washed out as well as portions of Route 3 in Lancaster.

Lightning struck a 4-story post and beam barn and ignited a fire that destroyed the barn and damaged the attached home.  The barn was filled with antiques which were consumed by the blaze.  The home sustained smoke and water damage.

Lightning struck the steeple of a local church and reportedly blew the steeple apart and right off the church.  Charred wooden boards were left strewn across the church lawn.  Although the lightning did not cause a fire, some damage was reported inside the church.

Low pressure strengthening off the New England coast brought strong west winds to much of southwest New Hampshire. Gusts were estimated near 60 mph across the higher elevations of Cheshire and western Hillsborough Counties, which brought down trees and power lines from Keene to Hillsborough. No injuries were reported.

Heavy rainfall associated with the remnants of Ivan caused flash flooding in Cheshire County. Storm totals of 3 to 5 inches brought a tributary of the Branch Brook out of its banks and flooded a nearby roadway. No injuries were reported.

A severe thunderstorm downed large trees and wires in Peterborough and Temple. A roadway was shut down for about one hour while debris was cleared. In Manchester, another severe thunderstorm downed large tree limbs and blew several picnic tables over.

A severe thunderstorm downed large trees and wires in Peterborough and Temple. A roadway was shut down for about one hour while debris was cleared. In Manchester, another severe thunderstorm downed large tree limbs and blew several picnic tables over.
A severe thunderstorm produced damaging winds which brought down numerous large trees and power lines in Charlestown New Hampshire.
A severe thunderstorm produced 3/4 inch hail in the town of Errol.
A severe thunderstorm downed several trees in Hinsdale, and downed a tree and wires in Swanzey.
Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in numerous trees down in a campground in Hampton Falls, on the evening ofAugust 20.  Several campers where damaged or destroyed, and 10 people sustained injuries.
Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in several reports of trees and wires down in Claremont on the afternoon of August 29.
Strong thunderstorm winds resulted in a large tree down, blocking Route 3 in Clarksville, just south of Pittsburg.
Thunderstorm winds brought trees down across Route 135 in Littleton on the evening of August 29.
Strong thunderstorm winds brought trees down in the town of Bath on the evening of August 29.
Strong thunderstorms resulted in trees down on a house in Columbia.
Lightning damaged or destroyed computer and radio equipment at the Sunapee Town Office building.  The lightning reportedly followed phone lines into the building.
Thunderstorm winds downed limbs and power lines on Moses Clark Road near Lisbon.
Thunderstorm winds blew down trees on North Littleton Road near Littleton.

Heavy snow blanketed far southwest New Hampshire, as low pressure tracked southeast of New England. Amounts averaging 6 inches were common throughout Cheshire County. Several accidents were reported due to the combination of icy roads and poor visibility.Some specific snowfall totals, as reported by trained spotters, included 7 inches in Keene, Winchester, and Rindge; and 6 inches in East Alstead.

Damaging winds affected the higher elevations of Cheshire County, as strengthening low pressure tracked across northern new England and a strong cold front moved through the region. There were several reports of downed trees and power lines. No injuries were reported.
